lesson.menu_desc: Handling objections and closing the deal -- persuade with the second conditional (If we lowered..., would you...?) -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 -- Negotiation II -- handling objections and closing the deal
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Nilo Mesquita Patussi | Aula 15 -- Negotiation II

listenings.text: Hello again, Doctor Nilo, Sofia here from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Last time we opened a negotiation and proposed terms. Today we go to the hard part: the other side pushes back, raises objections, and you have to close the deal. So here is some advice. First, never fight an objection. When the sponsor says the price is too high, or this clause is a deal breaker, stay calm and acknowledge their concern. Say, I understand your concern, or, that is a fair point. This lowers the tension. Second, ask a question to find the real problem behind the objection. Often the budget is not the only issue. Third, to persuade, use the second conditional. This is a softer, more hypothetical way to make an offer. You say, if we lowered the yearly fee, would you sign for four seasons, or, if we added two more categories, would you reconsider. Notice the pattern: if plus past tense, and would plus the base verb. It sounds polite and it makes the other side imagine the deal. Fourth, offer a small concession to get something bigger in return, but protect your deal breakers, the points you cannot give up. And finally, when you sense agreement, move to close. Say, so, do we have a deal, or, shall we finalize the terms today. Get them to sign off while the mood is good. Remember, Doctor, an objection is not a no. It is a door. Open it with a good question and a smart hypothetical offer. Good luck closing the deal.

Let me tell you how I closed my toughest sponsorship deal for Corinthians. We had already proposed our terms, but the marketing director came back with three objections. First, he said our yearly price was too high for his budget. I did not argue. I said, I understand your concern, that is a fair point, and then I asked a question to find the real issue. It turned out the timing was the problem, not the total value. So I tried a hypothetical offer. I said, if we spread the payment across the season, would that work better for your budget. He liked that idea. His second objection was a real deal breaker for us. He wanted our logo off the training kit, and that was a point I could not give up. So I protected it, but I offered a small concession somewhere else. I said, if we gave you exclusive rights in one extra category, would you keep our logo on the kit. He reconsidered, and he agreed. His last concern was a guarantee about audience numbers. I could not promise exact figures, so I stayed honest, but I offered a follow up review after six months. Slowly, every objection turned into a question, and every question turned into a small agreement. When I felt the mood was right, I moved to close. I said, so, do we have a deal, shall we finalize the terms today. He smiled and signed off. The whole time, my best tool was the second conditional. If we did this, would you do that. It let me test ideas without promising anything, and it kept both of us comfortable until the deal was done.
